Transcrição Completa: 3 Sales Follow Up and Leveraging Your Time with Jordan Stupar
[00:00] Thank you, Jeremy. I appreciate that. That's a nice intro. And yeah, when I've been talking to Jeremy, like, dude, it is depressing when I hear those types of stories with people spending a quarter million on ads and like making like $251,000 worth of closing because their closer suck.
[00:20] So, if there's anything that I can give you guys today, I didn't really know exactly what to lay out for you guys, but I talked to a couple of you guys yesterday. I was like, I'm talking tomorrow. what can I do to bring you some value?
[00:35] And uh really two things that I heard. One was follow-up, which is a very appropriate subject matter to study considering the fact that some people suck at closing deals. Follow-up is kind of like that end all be all 100% conversion ratio if you're consistent.
[01:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Follow-Up Black Book"
NÚMEROS: $700 (carteira Louis Vuitton), $1,200-1,300 (dinheiro em espécie)
VISUAL: Livro "Follow-Up Black Book" ao lado de carteira Louis Vuitton com dinheiro, chave de Porsche, layout comparativo de valor - conhecimento vs. bens materiais
[01:15] And I'm also going to dig into uh the best way that you guys can scale yourselves and leverage time like like really rich people do. and it's something that I've dedicated a lot of my time to uh over the last couple of years growing my business.
[01:45] Um, at the end of the day, if you don't know who I am, my name is Jordan Stupar. I live in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. How many of you guys have ever been to Wisconsin?
[02:30] Um, it's a great place, but it's too fucking cold. So, I'm going to probably get a spot down here sooner or later. I lived here for five years. That's how I know Jeremy.
[02:45] And, uh, yeah, I help people. I help companies increase sales. and I do it pretty well. But regardless, a lot of you guys referred to me yesterday as the card guy. So, I'm that guy now. I sell decks of cards for uh a lead generation purposes and I might be one of the only people that profitably generates leads and it's insane.
[03:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Which Would You Rather Have?"
NÚMEROS: $99 (preço do livro), $700+ (valor da carteira e dinheiro)
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre livro de follow-up ($99) versus carteira Louis Vuitton + dinheiro + chave de Porsche, layout de escolha binária
[03:30] But in this photo, there's a book that says the follow-up blackbooks, the techniques you've been looking for. And then there's a I don't know, $700 Louis wallet with, I don't know, $1,200, $300 bucks in cash and a Porsche key over there.
[03:45] And I always ask people, which one would you rather have like right now? What would you rather have? A $99 book that I sell or would you rather have the cash, the wallet, and the Porsche?
[04:00] And most people are like, well, I'd rather have the wallet, the cash, and the car. But the truth is is that if I can give you that book that helped me acquire the other things in this photo, well then that becomes more valuable.
[04:20] because follow-up is the thing that you can use on the front part of your pipeline, the backend part of your pipeline when prospects and clients go dark on you, current clients, making sure that they are communicative. Follow-up literally heals everything.
[04:40] So, if you happen to work with a team of closers or you're a closer yourself and you're wondering how you can immediately make more money without generating another lead, then all you have to do is look at your pipeline, work backwards a little bit, and you got to figure out how to actually follow up with people.
[05:00] And it took me a long time to figure out how to follow up properly. Obviously, we've all done that call once or twice where we're like, "Hey, you know, just just checking in, Omar, you know, like, did you get the proposal, bro? you know, are you thinking about moving forward with us?
[05:20] And then I call them again and I'm like, dude, just touching base, just trying to connect. And it's like, you never get those people to pick up the phone. You know that you're not providing them with any real value.
[05:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "They're Going to Buy From Somebody"
NÚMEROS: 6 dias, 6 semanas, 6 meses (timeline de compra)
VISUAL: Timeline visual mostrando janela de oportunidade de compra, conceito de inevitabilidade da decisão de compra
[05:50] And then you just give up on following up with them. And then you start blaming the leads. You start blaming whatever it is. And you just give up because you got another inbound lead.
[06:05] So, at the end of the day, the point that I want to make here is that every single person that you guys talk to about your products, your services, your marketing, whatever it is, they're going to buy something like your product or service sooner or later from somebody. Can we all agree upon that?
[06:25] If somebody's talking to you about digital marketing and they take a pass on you for right now, chances are pretty good in 6 days, 6 weeks, 6 months, sooner or later, they're going to pull the trigger on some type of marketing because they have a pain point that they were originally trying to solve with you.
[06:45] And of course, maybe it wasn't the right time or you didn't show enough value or the other million things that could go wrong in your sales process that I'm happy to do some Q&A with you guys, but ultimately they're going to buy from somebody else. Why not you?
[07:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ABC - Always Bring Value" (vs. "Always Be Closing")
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Contraste entre filosofia antiga (ABC - Always Be Closing) e nova abordagem (ABC - Always Bring Value), referência ao filme Glengarry Glen Ross
[07:20] That's the question. If you have the staying power to stay in front of somebody long enough for them to make that decision to work with you, why not stay in front of them?
[07:35] Now, the problem is is that most of us, dude, they don't teach us in school how to provide value. You guys have heard uh Glengary, Glenn Ross, Alec Baldwin, you know, ABC, always be closing.
[07:50] You know the movie. How many of you guys haven't seen that movie? Glengary, Glenn Ross, anybody? Okay, good. Well, now you now you know what to watch tonight. Quintessential sales movie.
[08:05] But the point is is that I want you guys to stop thinking about ABC. I want you to start thinking about ABC. Always bring value. If you can bring somebody value, dude, you can call them 20 times in a day. You can hit them 30 times in a month.
[08:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Bang Triangle"
NÚMEROS: 75% (entretenimento), 25% (educação)
VISUAL: Triângulo dividido mostrando proporção 75/25, conceito de estrutura de follow-up baseada em entretenimento e educação
[08:40] You can do whatever you want as often as you want because you're bringing something valuable. So, what I'm going to teach you guys is uh what I call the bang angle. This is obviously referenced upon a triangle, which you guys are familiar with since being like 2 years old or whenever you start learning shapes.
[09:00] But, but the point is is that in order to get somebody's business sooner or later, you need to do three things. The first thing that you're going to need to do is understand that follow-up is 75% entertainment.
[09:20] Jay Leno has a fucking amazing car collection worth I don't know probably 20 million bucks or more. And obviously that's just his car collection. Guy did pretty damn well in his life.
[09:35] What did Jay Leno do? Did he like invent something? Did he like start a big company like Facebook or something? Like, no, dude. He went on to the late night show and told jokes and entertained people. It's all he did. And he amassed a lot of wealth.
[09:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "75% Entertainment + 25% Education = Relationship"
NÚMEROS: 75%, 25%
VISUAL: Fórmula visual mostrando componentes do follow-up efetivo, equação de construção de relacionamento através de entretenimento e educação
[10:10] So, 75% entertainment. So, when you guys are thinking about following up with a prospect, stop thinking about following up about the deal or the reason why you're following up with them.
[10:25] I've sent out a lot of proposals in my time. And of course, when I call you, if I send you a proposal to do business and you don't call me back tomorrow or sign, if I call you, do you know why I'm calling you? To get the deal.
[10:40] Like, I don't need to pick up the phone and be like, "Yo, man, did you get the proposal?" Of course you got the proposal. I sent it to you. I got that Panda doc notification saying you opened it.
[10:55] Like I don't need to check up on that. But if I can check up on something that's valuable that I sent you. Well, then now I have a different reason to reach out.
[11:10] And it's like valuable. It's like I actually care about you. It's like I want to have a relationship with you. I want to get to know you.
[11:25] And so the second thing that you're going to be doing, and I'll dig into some actual like this is how you do it type shit. The other side of follow-up after 75% entertainment is 25% education.
[11:45] The reason why we want to entertain somebody isn't to like just make them laugh or smile. It's so that I can hold their attention throughout the follow-up process.
[12:00] If I can get you to be entertained by something that you're interested in, a hobby that you enjoy, a brand that you like, whatever, like you're going to consume my follow-up and we're going to be able to stay connected and I'm going to hold your attention throughout the follow-up process.
[12:20] Then of course I need to educate you a little bit more too since you failed to have be educated enough at the time of closing to actually make a deal because education when you're smart when you're educated you're informed and you have all the data and information you need to make a decision well then you can increase somebody's certainty level.

[16:20] And then I turn the camera over. I'm like, "Dude, Melissa, my guy Dale's marriage is in your hands. You said you were going to send the thing over, so send it over immediately, bro."
[16:35] She says, "Uh, Jordan and Dale, so funny. Go to date night, Dale. I'm going to sign it. Can we have the start date March 14th, Melissa Glass Designs?" And boom, we got a deal.
[16:50] Like 5 minutes later, we got a signed contract. And uh, of course, like that's dope because it was $62,500 off of a stupid video that I took in my office. That's what entertainment is.

[22:30] I can't send a selfie video of Dale sitting on a couch for eight hours a day out to everybody that needs one. I want to be able to help identify who to follow up with.
[22:50] because at the end of the day, you have two forms of activities that you guys can do. You have scalable activities. Boom. hit a button, mass email out to a 100,000 people.
[23:05] That's a pretty scalable activity because I'm pretty good on the phones, but I can never make a 100,000 calls or follow-up calls in a day. So, that would be a scalable activity.
[23:20] A non-scalable activity is going to be the thing that you do like sending a video, creating a special meme, writing up a personalized email, handwritten note, whatever. Those non-scalable activities.
[23:40] But there's people in your pipeline that deserve non-scalable activities and there's people in your pipeline that don't deserve your non-scalable activities and the time that it takes to get things done.
[24:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "HubSpot Lead Scoring System"
NÚMEROS: 110 (lead score alto), -10 (lead score negativo), 25,000+ (total de contatos no CRM)
VISUAL: Sistema de pontuação de leads no HubSpot, exemplos de scores positivos e negativos, dashboard de priorização de leads
[24:15] So, one of the things I did I'm not recommending, well, I would, but I switched over to HubSpot for a couple of reasons. One of them is the fact that they have a marketing hub, they have a sales hub, and a bunch of other shit.
[24:35] But essentially, I've been uh figuring out how to nurture a mass population of people in my CRM, which is I don't know like 25,000 21 something like that. How do I nurture all these people?
[24:50] And how can I best figure out who to make a phone call to? Because that's a non-scalable activity. Who needs a personalized email? That's a non-scalable activity.
[25:05] And so out of these two, I have a thing called a lead score. And the higher the lead score, the warmer you are, more likely you'll be to probably like do business or book a call or whatever.
[25:25] And we have different thresholds of all this, but essentially this lead right here is 110 lead score. He better fucking close this next week because we sent out a proposal on Friday.
[25:40] And that lead score indicates that I can bank on the fact that he's probably going to go ahead and do that cuz it lead score is tied into page views on my website, uh, talk time on the phone with one of my guys, literally like a bunch of different things.
[26:00] If you clicked on one of my ads, it's even it drives up that lead score for me to know who to spend the most time on from a non-scalable perspective. You guys with me so far?
[26:15] Or would you guys rather send a selfie video and make a call and send a personalized email to Samuel over here who's a negative 10? I'll take Jason, too. That's the smart choice, sir. Good job.

[27:25] I'd much rather have them calling warm leads in the 30s and 40s and then hot leads in the 50s to hundreds.

[29:20] That information from Shopify goes right into HubSpot. Um, and actually I didn't even need to use Zapier, which is fucking dope because I'm not a Zapier expert. I'm not like you guys or whatever. But it has a native integration.
[29:40] So boom, all of your information goes straight into Shopify. It says deal closed in the e-commerce pipeline for this much. Here's your order number.

[36:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lead Score Threshold & Round Robin"
NÚMEROS: 30 pontos (threshold para distribuição), 9 horas (jornada de trabalho típica)
VISUAL: Sistema de distribuição automática de leads para equipe de vendas, algoritmo de round robin baseado em score e tipo de lead, priorização de oportunidades
[36:50] So, they're on a drip sequence of emails and if they click on this one thing and it segments in them into business owners and if they don't, it goes there and if they book a meeting, lead score goes up.
[37:10] And the whole idea of these marketing emails that sent get sent out is just to drive up a lead score and bring people back to my website because that's bonus points because you made it back to my blog page, YouTube video, whatever, just continues driving it up.
[37:30] And based on which page you land on my website, there might be a little bit more intent. So, if you fill out a a form on my website, lead score jumps. If you buy another deck of cards or something else, lead score jumps because now you're a repeat customer.
[37:50] I want to track and monitor all of this. Then, well, obviously I mentioned I'm driving up a lead score so that I can figure out who to follow up with and how to follow up with them.
[38:10] The next thing that happens once they reach a specific threshold, I think it's uh I want to say it's 30 30 points on the lead score. It gets round-robined to the the correct person on my team.
[38:30] Uh depending on what type of lead you are, you might end up with like my newest guy that literally like is going to call you and be like, "It's uh so and so over here. I'm the new guy."
[38:45] Because you're just like a little baby deal. But if you're like a big deal CEO, $6 billion company, you just bought 20 decks of cash cards, you opened up all the emails, dude, that's going to me.
[39:05] I'm not going to trust anybody on my team with the big whale deals. So, I have the correct lead distribution set up.
[39:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Kixie Dialer Integration"
NÚMEROS: 4 linhas simultâneas (power dialing), 300 calls por dia (meta), 4 horas (talk time mínimo)
VISUAL: Sistema de discagem Kixie integrado ao HubSpot, capacidade de discar múltiplas linhas, recursos de whisper coaching, gravação de chamadas, SMS
[39:35] And this is where we start doing a little bit, a tiny bit of the non-scalable stuff where you would actually have to go into that contact and subscribe them to a sequence.
[39:50] A sequence is completely different from marketing. That's all from directly from the salesperson or the owner of that contact.
[40:05] And that's where, you know, it's like, "Hey, it's Dale over here from Jordan's office. Know that you got the cards about a week ago. Have you had a chance to check them out? I would love to connect and learn a little bit more about your business."
[40:25] And depending on their behavior there drives up the lead score, whatever. But after two emails from my sales guy, they get scheduled a task to actually call that person.
[40:40] So, it's been several days, they've gotten several emails, they've been warmed up pretty good, and now they actually get a phone call from somebody because that again is a non-scalable activity.
[41:00] We also know in Wisconsin that it's actually going to get warm when those round red when those robins actually come back into town. It's It's always nice to see those birds.
[41:15] Uh, anyway, so you get uh put into a sequence with phone calls, emails. Uh, we just integrated a dialer called Kixie. If you do any type of outbound, I would heavily recommend it. It is badass and I would recommend it to any of your clients as well that may be using any type of dialer.
[41:40] From there, dude, you go through our sales process and we get money from you.
[41:55] Kixie is a dialer and uh essentially you can load it up and you can make phone calls on it, whisper coaching, call recording, like everything. It pushes all the call notes and everything into the CRM with all the data.
[42:15] It's also uh does text messaging and such and I'm going to actually dig a little bit more into that here in a second if you're if you're interested.
[42:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Plumbing Company Video Example"
NÚMEROS: $60,000 (valor do contrato), 7 minutos (tempo de resposta após vídeo)
VISUAL: Exemplo de vídeo customizado de follow-up para empresa de encanamento, Jordan com desentupidor no banheiro, humor específico da indústria
[42:45] Uh, so this is my flow and it requires a bit of patience because when you buy a deck of cards, dude, like you're you're there looking at like my logo still like I want to call you right then and there. I want to shoot you a text right then and there.
[43:05] But again, that's not scalable because I'm not going to sit in front of my computer all day every day while ads are running and conversions are happening.
[43:20] So regardless of what type of inbound or outbound flow you have, take a moment. My challenge for you this weekend or throughout the week is to take a step back from the machine that you've built and ask yourself where can I optimize this and what types of tools or technology can I implement into my process that's going to help me identify how to spend my time?
[43:50] Because a lot of you guys are entrepreneurs, you're you're marketers, you're closers, you've got something professional enough going on to be here. And at the end of the day, one of the things that I know that all of us have a problem with is some form of time management.
[44:10] Would you guys all agree that that's probably something you could get better at? Great. I'm going to talk a little bit more about that in a second based on the improvements I've been able to make professionally in my own career.
[44:30] But at the end of the day, you've got the non-scalable activities. Again, this is a another video that's not going to play. I was working on another $60,000 contract with a plumbing company out in Los Angeles and they weren't getting back to us at all.
[44:50] They loved us. We're going to do business with you. We're going to sign, I promise, nothing. How many of you guys have had that happen over and over and over again?
[45:05] So, instead of like doing sitting on the couch waiting for date night, they're plumbers. So, this whole video, that's uh one of my sales guys. He's like, "Where's Jordan? I wonder where he's at. I think he went to the bathroom.
[45:25] So, he goes into the office bathroom and he actually finds me in the stall with a plunger plunging the toilet and he's like, "Jordan, what are you doing?"
[45:40] And I'm like, "What? Oh, dude, this is a little embarrassing, man. I'm in in the bathroom here. Dude, I'm trying to get the contract back from Pride Plumbing."
[45:55] Now, I figured since they're plumbers, they probably don't really use the internet that often, and they probably sent the contract through the pipes. So, I'm trying to get the contract from Michael at Pride Plumbing.
[46:15] And we we sent that in a text, dude. 7 minutes later, $60,000 contract shows up with a bunch of laughing emojis and shit and we just got paid. And then we took that company and we we tripled their revenue in like seven months. It was psychotic.

NÚMEROS: 50 deals (pipeline exemplo), 25 deals (após 50% de perda), 10% (taxa de conversão estimada de 20,000 leads)
VISUAL: Conceito de "chocar a vida" de cada oportunidade, filosofia de push up or push out, gerenciamento agressivo de pipeline
[56:05] So, I would highly encourage you guys to take a step back and write down where some breaks might be in your overall sales process and how you might know that you're wasting your time.
[56:20] because nothing is more expensive than lost deals and wasted time. And if I'm going to lose deals, I would rather spend all of that time getting deals throughout the follow-up process and making sure that I stay in front of people.
[56:40] The point is is that you want to choke the life out of every real opportunity that's in your sales funnel. And also with your scalable stuff, dude, I want to basically email you enough where like, dude, you go deeper into my funnel or you get out of the funnel.
[57:00] I want I want you to do that before I ever even call you because I'd love to pick up the phone and be like, "Hey, it's Josh calling from Stupar's office and be like, "Dude, I just followed you on Instagram, man. I've been following Jordan since I bought the cars and those are fucking awesome."
[57:20] Blah, blah, blah. It's like, now that's a high quality lead. That's somebody worth talking to. That's somebody that's likely to give us that second money, that third money, and actually allow us to help change the trajectory of their business.
[57:40] And so don't be shy and don't be scared of annoying people following up with them whether it's scalable or non-scalable because the whole point like I was telling him even with my my own people you're going to push people up or you're going to push them out.
[58:00] I'm not a good fit for every single person that bought a deck of cash cards. I'm just not. It's not a reality for me to believe that. It's a reality to think that I don't know 10% of that 20,000 probably about ready to do something with me here in the next 60 days.
[58:25] So I can forecast that because that's probably probably fair to say 200 people end up rolling with with a deal.

[71:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Four Quadrants - Bottom Right"
NÚMEROS: Quadrante 1 de 4
VISUAL: Primeiro quadrante: atividades que você é RUIM e ODEIA fazer (exemplo: impostos, planilhas, contabilidade, folha de pagamento), prioridade máxima de delegação
[71:20] So, how do you know when it's time to hire somebody else, delegate something, and stop doing things that don't make you happy or don't actually make you money?
[71:35] Those that that's basically the conversation that I want to have. I paid uh what was it 20 $30,000 for some business coaching. I can't take full credit for this uh idea. This came from uh Dan Sullivan, just a very prolific uh business and entrepreneurial coach.
[72:00] Um, if you're into that type of stuff, check out Dan Sullivan. Uh, this was one of the lessons that they gave me, and I'll paraphrase the best that I can.
[72:15] Uh, but this really helped me because at the time when I signed up for their program for for business and entrepreneurship, I was a solopreneur. I didn't have a ton of capital yet behind me or a lot of recurring revenue. I didn't even have cash cards to be totally fair.
[72:40] And so, I I needed to figure out like, dude, how do I know when it's the right time to hire somebody or do something or get something done in my business?
[72:55] And so ultimately I took this lesson and you can divide everything that you do. And by the way, I would challenge you guys to this week or whenever you're going to do some homework or reflection on what you learned here this weekend.
[73:15] is I want you guys to write down everything that it is that you do in your business. I don't care if you spend 10 minutes a week copywriting. Write that down. That's something that you're doing and spending your time on.
[73:35] I don't care if you're spending 12 hours in a day shooting one ad video cuz you suck on camera. that's something that you're doing for 12 hours that week and something blah blah blah you might want to get better at.
[73:55] But essentially, I want you to divide all of the shit in your business and in your life into four quadrants. And at the bottom right quadrant, you guys might want to actually do this on a sheet of paper at some period of time.
[74:15] is in the bottom right quadrant, write down the things or the hats that you wear, the activities that you do in your business day-to-day that you fucking suck at and you hate doing.
[74:35] For me, that's taxes, that's spreadsheets, that's accounting, that's payroll, that's all of that financial stuff. Like, dude, I like making money and I like watching the bank account grow, but I'm not the guy that's going to itemize my expenses for my AMX card. Like, you got to be kidding me. I ain't going to do that shit.
[75:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Four Quadrants - Bottom Left"
NÚMEROS: Quadrante 2 de 4, 8 horas/dia (exemplo de fulfillment)
VISUAL: Segundo quadrante: atividades que você é BOM mas ODEIA fazer (exemplo: cold calling, fulfillment de produtos, tarefas operacionais), segunda prioridade de delegação
[75:15] So, that was the very first thing I got off my plate when I was a solopreneur is I outsourced to bookkeeper and CPA so that they can do Quickbooks and they can just manage this stuff. so I can have some clean books and I can minimize uh my tax um my taxes. So that was for me. It was immediately that.
[75:40] Then it turned into dude I I hate fulfilling eight hours a day decks of cash cards, putting them into bubble mailers, printing off your sticker, putting it on the envelope, going to the post office, and doing that whole song and dance.
[76:00] So then my next hire before hiring anybody in my business was somebody that could work in my little shipping office that could actually do the fulfillment and process everything out of Shopify.
[76:20] so that I could get eight hours of my time back every day and contact these leads, nurture them. What's up? How's it going? Did you like my product? Just being a responsible business owner looking for a little bit of feedback.
[76:40] By the way, could I shoot you an email tomorrow about something else that we're up to? Yeah, cool. So I'm in the sales process.
[76:55] Then I have to realize the other quadrant next to that is you're going to write down the things that you're good at but you hate doing.
[77:10] I'm a sales guy through and through, bro. Like I can crush cold calls. I can dial a fuckload of people. But guess what? I'm just like you guys and I hate picking up the phone. I'm good at it, but I hate it. It's a horrible, horrible waste of my time.
[77:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cardone Office Story"
NÚMEROS: $500,000/ano (salário anterior), 2-3 vendedores (pedido para contratar), 2016 (ano)
VISUAL: História de deixar escritório do Cardone, vivendo em yacht club, dirigindo Porsche 911, pedido negado para contratar equipe de cold calling
[77:50] I knew this back in 2016 and my last sales job. One of the reasons why I quit Cardone's office wasn't because I wasn't making a lot of money. I was making 500 grand a year living at a yacht club, driving a Porsche 911 that I bought. Like, life was pretty good.
[78:15] But I realized, dude, I'm getting more valuable pitching and closing deals than I am having to figure out where all my appointments are.
[78:30] So, I actually asked um the executive staff like, "Dude, can I hire with my own money two, three salespeople that can do all the cold calls for me, line up my calendar with some good qualified appointments so I can just spend my whole 8, 10 hours a day closing deals?"
[78:55] And they said, "No." And I said, "Fuck you. I'm out." Um, that's the short story.
[79:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Four Quadrants - Top Right"
NÚMEROS: Quadrante 3 de 4, 3 horas/semana (exemplo de lavanderia)
VISUAL: Terceiro quadrante: atividades que você é BOM e NÃO SE IMPORTA de fazer (exemplo: landing pages, edição de vídeo), terceira prioridade de delegação
[79:25] But the point is is that in your business, there are things that you're pretty good at, but you hate doing. Don't do them. It doesn't make a lot of sense to do something just because you're kind of good at it.
[79:45] For me, in my business, that was uh video editing. I shouldn't say that I hate it, but like it's time-consuming. Any of you guys that have ever used Final Cut, Adobe, whatever, like making a banger of a video, like it's pretty time-consuming no matter what.
[80:10] And so that's why I created Stuart Media Group, outsourced all my shit and why we have a lot of clients that send us all their shit and they get it chopped up. We I don't have to spend my time doing that.
[80:30] something that I'm pretty good at because I've spent a lot of time doing it, but again, it's not the right thing for me to be doing and spending my valuable time on as a CEO and as a business owner.
[80:50] So, write down a list sometime this week whenever you reflect on the lessons that you learned this week and write down the things that you're good at but hate doing.
[81:10] Then, you're going to want to shift your attention to things that you're good at but you don't mind doing. For me, uh that's building out a landing page.
[81:25] Like I'm pretty good at building out a landing page with a little copy, you know, a little explainer about what I do and making a little VSSL and I know enough to throw a tracking code in the header and you know, like I get it. I get it, dude.
[81:45] Like, and also I like the creative process and I like to figure out how to challenge people when they're reading the copy or when they go from here to there on the page. Like, I get it and I kind of enjoy it.
[82:05] So, that's a hard thing for me to do business with at times because I don't really mind it and I kind of enjoy it and I've I've done millions of dollars on the landing page that I built for cash cards myself. I got a little I got a little bit of an ego now, but I shouldn't be doing it.
[82:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Four Quadrants - Top Left (GOAL)"
NÚMEROS: Quadrante 4 de 4, 100% do tempo (objetivo)
VISUAL: Quarto quadrante (objetivo final): atividades que NINGUÉM FAZ MELHOR QUE VOCÊ (exemplo: palestras, coaching, criar conteúdo, VSLs), onde você deve passar 100% do tempo
[82:45] It's not something that I need to be spending my valuable time on because in that third quadrant, there's things that you enjoy doing more and there's somebody out there that can do it better than what you're doing.
[83:05] I know there's probably a handful of you guys in this room that could fucking whoop my ass when it comes to a landing page for conversion or for a funnel or whatever because that's what you do do. I don't do do that. I just kind of do it and I'm kind of good at it.
[83:30] So, I need to make a decision as a business owner. Hey, how do I get to this quadrant? And this is the main question all of you guys need to be asking yourselves is how do I operate 100% of my time in this top left quadrant.
[83:50] and focus 100% of your time on the one thing or several things in your business that literally nobody can do better than you.
[84:05] For me, that would be getting on a plane, coming down and doing some speaking. Nobody in my business can do that better than me. Nobody in my business can help explain sales to anybody else.
[84:25] I should be on coaching calls. I should be in front of a camera. I should be making VSSLs. I should be doing YouTube. I should be slamming social media with content every fucking day. And that's all I should be doing.
[84:45] I shouldn't be thinking about a Shopify customer using that little chat on my homepage because they want a discount for 10 10% off because can I get 10% off? I don't need to be managing that.
[85:05] I don't need to be managing fulfillment. I don't need to be managing making all the calls. I don't need to be managing my landing pages. I don't need to be managing my video editing. I don't need to be managing any of that shit.
[85:25] The only thing that I should be doing are the things in my business that literally nobody can do better than me, even if I hired them.
[85:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Live This Way - Delegation Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: 300% crescimento ano a ano, 20 meses (tempo para construir negócio de $3M), $3 milhões/ano (receita atual)
VISUAL: Filosofia de viver focado no top left quadrant, resultados: 300% crescimento YoY, $3M em 20 meses, conceito de scaling através de delegação radical
[85:55] So, think about what are the things in your business that you legitimately do better than anybody else in your business and better than anybody else that you could possibly hire. work on that skill, spend time doing that thing, and like legitimately, I think my next slide says it, live this way. Like live this way.
[86:20] Now, I'm going to I'm going to let you guys know, there's some of you guys that have outsourced closing teams. Some of you guys have tried to do it yourselves. Maybe some of you guys have done it yourselves.
[86:35] Regardless, maybe that's something that you should outsource and hire out for because there's somebody out there that can do it better than you and somebody that can actually convert.
[86:50] And so that's a real question in your business. And of course, all of these different things, delegating all of these different things from things that you suck at and hate, from things that you're not that you're good at but hate doing, things that you're good at but don't hate.
[87:10] Get all of that off your plate. Maybe you can't afford to make those investments right now, but don't start up here. Start down there so that you can get the things that you actually hate off your plate and get somebody to do it.
[87:30] Dude, I have a girl in my office that literally loves fucking QuickBooks. Like, dude, she comes in on Monday morning with her little fedora on. She's like, "Fuck yeah." Logs in. She's like organizing everything.
[87:50] I'm like, I literally can't believe that somebody out there actually enjoys it. So, give other people the opportunity to do the shit that you hate because there are people out there that will actually enjoy it. Like, it's real.
[88:10] And so, get all of that off your plate so that you can do the most valuable thing in your business. And I promise you, you'll have results like mine where you're able to grow 300% year-over-year because you're doing the most valuable thing that you need to do.
[88:35] And guess what? That is where all the top-line revenue really comes from in your business.
[88:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Marketing Client Example"

VISUAL: Exemplo de cliente de marketing subvalorizado, comparação de pricing, discussão sobre percepção de valor vs. ROI puro
[89:05] Now, another thing that I do want to suggest for those of you that are doing marketing, I just acquired another customer uh for sales training. He runs a marketing company.
[89:20] We jumped on a call. He says, "Jordan, I charge $3,000 upfront and $1,200 a month uh for for advertising for building stuff on my white labeled GoHigh Level and building funnels and landing pages and all this shit."
[89:40] I'm like, "You run ads, too?" He's like, "Yeah, I run ads." I'm like, "Okay, cool." Like, "Bro, first things first. Fuck your prices. Like, you don't respect yourself. You really don't. At 1,200 bucks a month, I would not even fucking consider hiring you."
[90:05] And he was trying to pitch me. I was like, just based on price alone, I already know that, bro, that's going to be useless in my business. I need to hire somebody else with a little bit more clout, a better authority.
[90:20] But if I could only afford 1,200 bucks, dude, that's a great starting point. But the thing I want you guys to understand is what I explained to him is he said, "Dude, I'm just trying to get people ROI. How many of you guys like getting your customers ROI?" Fucking yeah.
[90:45] But imagine for a moment that that's not the reason why most people do business with you. Let that sink in real quick. It's not always about ROI or about the little deliverables and all that, dude.

VISUAL: Cálculo de ROI baseado em valor do tempo, não em ROI de marketing, conceito de "comprar tempo de volta", matemática de $35k de tempo vs. $10k de investimento
[91:20] Maybe it's just the fact that you're you've got Jordan Stupar on the horn who likes making landing pages and is pretty decent at them and doesn't necessarily care if you can get a 3% higher, you know, return on advertising.

[91:55] So that that helps me make sense of the fact that I can trip and fall over the investment for you to do my landing pages for me.
[92:10] But understand that I'm not necessarily hiring you as a marketer to get an ROI. My ROI comes on me being able to put my time and energy in my business on things that nobody else can do better than me.
[92:30] And that's something you can explain to your customers. And I swear when you do, you will help unlock a lot of real reasons why people will hire you.

[93:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sell The Way You Buy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito fundamental: vender da mesma forma que você compra, eliminar objeções que você mesmo usa, mentalidade de comprador confiante
[94:05] So in your sales process now, you don't always have to focus on rorowaz and ROIs and blah blah blah. It's like, dude, some people just literally want you to be mo make them the most effective campaign uh to build a Facebook audience.
[94:25] I don't know. That's a pretty valuable thing. You own those people and you don't even necessarily have to pay to get in front of all of them.
[94:40] You know, if I had a hund a million followers on Facebook on my page, dude, like I put publish something, put zero money behind it, and probably get at least 10,000 people to look at it.
[94:55] That's more than I got right now. That might be worth a couple of bucks to me because I don't really know how to do an awareness campaign. But maybe you do better, and that's why I would hire you. It's not really an an ROI. It's just an end result that I'm going for.
[95:15] So think on the things that people are really buying when they decide to work with you. And ultimately live your life in the same exact approach.
[95:30] Because when you sell the way that you buy, you don't leave a lot of room for other people to influence why they shouldn't or why they can't.
[95:45] That's a huge take-home point for you guys. A lot of you guys that might be struggling closing, it's because you don't buy shit. It's because you forgo on making proper investments.
[96:05] If you're always talking to your spouse, don't be shocked that you're having an impossible time closing other people when they're like, "Yeah, dude. Offer sounds good. Thanks for spending some time, but I got to talk to my wife."
[96:25] You're going to hear the same objections, stalls, and reasons why people shouldn't do business with you as the ones that you use in your own personal life.

[97:15] That's why I don't need to think about shit. I don't research anything. If I want it, I buy it. Done. And what I do is I go home to my wife and I say, "Hey, I just spent $54,000 today." Oh my god. On what? Zoom info. Illuminati shit.

[101:40] But it's my follow-up that's going to allow me to show that I'm fucking serious and I'm good at what I do.
[101:55] Imagine the Girl Scout girl shows up to your door. Knock knock knock. You go out. Little whatever 12-year-old girl's out there. I got I got cookies for sale. No, I'm good, man. I'm I'm on a I'm on a bit of a cleanse over here. Go away.
[102:15] She shows up tomorrow. You want some cookies, dude? I told you I'm on a diet. Go away. Okay, maybe you don't kick the Girl Scout girl. Next day, Girl Scout shows back up. I got cookies. You want some? I'm good.
[102:35] Sooner or later, she's going to come to your door every single day, dude. Sooner or later, you are going to buy cookies. In fact, you'll probably place the whole order and buy all of them so she never shows up again cuz she's sold out.
[102:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Murfreesboro Toyota Story"
NÚMEROS: $36,000 (valor do contrato), $1,000/mês por 36 meses, 2.5 anos (duração do follow-up), $5,000 (comissão), segunda-feira a sábado (frequência de follow-up)
VISUAL: História épica de follow-up: 2.5 anos de emails e voicemails diários, nunca repetir mensagem, zero respostas até contrato assinado e pago integralmente
[103:10] Same thing has happened to me in follow-up. I remember back in my last sales job, I sent out a $36,000 contract to a car dealership in Murfreesboro, Tennessee. Great town. I'm just kidding. There's literally nothing there except for like a KFC in a dirty like Lita Inn or something. Serious.
[103:35] And uh I start following up with this guy. It's $1,000 a month for 36 month three-year contract. And uh dude, literally I fuck you guys not. Two and a half years.
[103:55] Every single day, Monday through Saturday, I'm in the office. I send him an email and a couple hours later I call him and I leave a voicemail for two and a half years. I never quit. I never gave up. I never sent him the same email. Never left him the same voicemail.
[104:20] And I never ever got a response from this guy. Never. Not even lol. Nothing. Two and a half years later, dude, I get an email on a Tuesday before my like, you know, job really starts. It's like 7:30 in the morning.
[104:40] Signed contract from Murfreesboro Toyota. I'm like, "What?" I open it up. Signed, AMX and a note paid in full. Run the whole fucking card. I don't need this $1,000 a month for 36 month thing.
[105:00] And then in the email body, he said, "Never call me again. Never call me again. Don't send me your product. Don't onboard me. Don't do shit. Take me off your list. Delete me from everywhere. You did your job. You earned it. Here's whatever your commission is. I hope it's good.
[105:25] I earned $5,000 that day based on the fact that for two and a half years, John, my man, it's been two and a half years, bro. I'm literally going to die. Like do the deal, bro. Sooner or later, you did the deal.

[114:40] That was a point that I just proved to my team on Wednesday. They come in from 9 until 6. Sometimes they leave at 6:00. Sometimes they stay till 7. Sometimes they stay till 8. Sometimes they're feeling awesome, they'll stay till 9 or 10. and like do their thing.
[115:00] I'm like, "Bitch, you made 253 calls today and you were in the office for nine hours. I know you didn't work today, so why'd you even show up?"
[115:20] So, here's the other thing that you guys can do with your sales teams or the clients that you work with or whatever is, dude, hold everybody accountable to your expectations. It's your life. It's your business. It's the way that you want to run things. Why are you putting up with so much average shit?
[115:45] On Wednesday, I walked into my office because we had an issue. I I allowed my team to just do less. We got new CRM. We just changed dialers. We got to make sure the lead list is good. [116:10] And on Wednesday at 3:00, I went out on the sales floor with my computer. I said, "You fucking going to learn today." And I lit him up, bro.
[116:25] I looked at the little leaderboard on my TV in my office, bro. I had more phone calls, more talk time, and more deals at all of them by six o'clock. And I started working at three o'clock.

[117:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Time Management Reality Check"
NÚMEROS: 15 anos (diferença de experiência entre Jordan e outros), 2 anos (tempo no negócio de alguns participantes)
VISUAL: Conceito de que pessoas bem-sucedidas não estão acima, estão à frente, diferença entre posição e experiência, importância do tempo no jogo
[117:20] So the question is is how much of your time is being spent on the things that nobody can do better than you? And if and when needed, when can you go back to some of the other things that maybe you shouldn't be doing to offer correction and leadership to the people that now have that job?
[117:45] And if you can answer that question, you can get really realistic on what it is that you're doing with your time and all the wins that you're getting from it.
[118:00] you'll have a good idea of what your priorities need to be as a business owner, an entrepreneur, as a person with clients, and a lot of things on your plate because a lot of you guys have a lot on your plate.
[118:20] Especially those of you that are a little bit younger in the game. Not even talking about age. I'm just talking about how long you've been doing what you're doing.
[118:35] Jeremy's got nine, eight years under his belt. Dude, that's a long time to set up systems, processes, meet the right people, figure and figure out the game.
[118:50] Some of you guys maybe just started something a year ago, two years ago. Dude, Jeremy's not like above you. He's just ahead of you.
[119:05] So, be thinking on that, too, when you're looking at other more successful people. They've learned how to leverage their time. They've learned their skill set. And dude, they've spent way more time in the game than you have, and they just kind of have it that way now.
[119:30] So, that's where I look at things. I'm like, dude, you've been in the game 15 years longer than I am. I shouldn't be where you're at. It'd be nice if I was like that'd be dope, but like I got to do work this time.
[119:50] And so I want you guys to get excited about delegation. I want you guys to really get excited about spending some additional money on the people that can do whatever it is that you're not doing well enough that they can do it better.
[120:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Commission Structure"

VISUAL: Estrutura de comissão generosa: 50% do primeiro mês + 15% recurring, incentivo para nurturing de longo prazo, estrutura para outbound vs. inbound
[120:25] your question. Um, do you have you said a day off time with those 300 dials? Are your sales reps also like deals closed? Yes. As well as those 300 or else I'd have them make like a thousand calls a day if that's all they were really doing.

[122:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Building Thick Skin - UFC Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado (referência à luta de Ali)
VISUAL: Analogia com UFC e tomar socos, conceito de que resistência vem de apanhar repetidamente, nada realmente acontece em vendas comparado a luta física
[122:45] Yeah, just like like that fear of rejection when you just want to like make those, you know, those outbound calls, but you're just like, man, like the thick skin the thick skin comes with actually just taking the punches, you know?
[123:05] I was watching UFC last night, you know, and I watched these guys beat the fuck out of each other. One guy, I forgot his name, Ali Weatherwood. Yeah, dude. He took like an ankle like like hard as fuck right to the right to the jaw.
[123:25] I'm like, "Bro, he he just like you didn't feel that?" Like Jeremy and I are like, "What the hell?" Like, "Guy just got rocked."
[123:40] The thick skin just comes from getting punched in the face a bunch of times and understanding that dude, it doesn't fucking matter. You just got punched in the face. If it didn't knock you on the canvas, if it didn't concuss you, like nothing really happened.
[124:00] And particularly with what we're doing, it's not like somebody's reaching through the phone and like rocking your world like right in your nose. Like worst thing that happens is you maybe get cursed out and then hung up on. Like that is all that can actually happen.
[124:20] And that is by the way not a good enough reason for me to stop calling you because I believe in my shit more than you believe that I'm just a nuisance to you or I'm just whatever. Like somebody here has to have more conviction than the other and it ain't fucking you. So that's bullet point number one.
[124:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ripple Effect of Cold Calling"
NÚMEROS: 7 anos (follow de Instagram desde cold call), 60% (audiência do evento de pessoas de cold calls antigos), 10,000 seguidores (primeiros seguidores de cold calling)
VISUAL: Efeito cascata de cold calling: conexões de 2015 comparecendo a eventos em 2023, construção de audiência através de cold calls, valor de longo prazo de cada conexão
[125:00] Bullet point number two is understanding the actual ripple effect of doing as much as you possibly can in the shortest period of time as possible.
[125:15] Uh I don't I posted this little quick video on YouTube. Man, I just uh sold out of an event of like 50 people in Chicago two weekends ago. events scare the fuck out of me because it's like dude who's gonna pay give up their Saturday and learn about fucking sales like I'm like and you got to pay for the venue and all this shit.
[125:40] But anyway, I sold it out 50 people. Um and ultimately, dude, like 90% of the people that came to that event weren't from Chicago and weren't from Wisconsin. Like people came from all over the country, California. Jeremy and his team came up from Miami, yada yada.
[126:05] And literally, I'm literally looking at people in the audience who I've been connected with on social media for years. And it's not like I just followed you randomly. It's like I cold called you back in 2015.
[126:25] We kind of broed out and you're like, I'm not really a good prospect right now, dude. I'm just like top sales guy at the car dealership and like I'm not really looking to make a $5,000 purchase, man, but you sound like badass, too.
[126:45] And it's like, well, dude, what's your instep? And like, dude, that's how I built like the first like 10,000 followers was just like, "What's your Insta?" And I would follow people and they would follow me.
[127:05] And bro, those people still follow me. And I'm looking out at this audience that I have in Chicago, bro. And like 60% of these people are people that I haven't really spoken to, DM'd back, or had much of a conversation with.
[127:25] but they have been following long enough and they finally decided to buy a ticket and come to Chicago. And that was based on a cold call from seven years ago.
[127:45] So understand what the real ripple effect is of connecting with that one person that tells you to fuck off. Make it the worst case scenario.

VISUAL: Analogia de $1,440 na carteira representando minutos do dia, roubo de $2 não afeta o resto, conceito de não deixar rejeições arruinarem o dia

[128:40] Now you are gonna feel my rat, bitch. Now you're in five of my auto dialers with 10 people dialing this shit all day long. Good luck. It's like, dude, who cares? Like, I don't care if you curse me out. Like, it's happened so many times, bro. It's like, dude, it doesn't reflect on anything.

[129:25] Does that have anything to do with the money you got left in your wallet or your ability to spend it or what you would feel about it? Like, yeah, you would feel pissed for a quick second. I stole $2, but does it ruin the rest of the money in your wallet? No.
[129:45] So, if you hang up on me, dude, on to the next. Fuck you, too. And I'm just going to call the next person because all I'm looking for I tell my team this all the time, dude. I'm looking for four people to have a halfway decent conversation with today.

[131:50] That guy. Yeah. Like, dude, I just bought back like 10 more minutes of my life. Thank you, God.
[132:05] The only reason why we ever feel the sting of rejection is because that that girl or that guy, whatever, was the only person in our pipeline. And that's why some of you guys don't want to pick up the phone BECAUSE YOU ONLY GOT one in the pipeline. You only got one for April.
[132:30] But if you have 50 working deals right now because you actually picked up the phone and you did the quantity of work necessary to get those deals in your pipeline. [133:20] and then get back to work prospecting and or generating leads or whatever you need to do. So, that's the way that you really overcome that is just work a lot. Take a lot of punches in the face.

[145:40] Um for instance, you might choose the $5,000 a month guy because he says, "I'm going to actually get monetization. [150:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Time Value Calculation Method"
NÚMEROS: $250,000/ano (exemplo), ÷12 meses, ÷30 dias, ÷24 horas = $14.82/hora, 6 horas/mês em ads = milhares de dólares em tempo
VISUAL: Método de cálculo de valor por hora: renda anual ÷ 12 ÷ 30 ÷ 24, aplicação para calcular custo real de fazer tarefas vs. terceirizar
[151:05] Something that you guys can actually conversate with your clients on is figuring out how much did uh did they earn last year? That business owner, that entrepreneur, that person that you're trying to work with and do some is have that discussion.
[151:30] How much did you take home last year last year? And if you ask it like that and a bit more of a command was with that down tone in the end of the question so it doesn't sound like a question, you're probably going to get a get a response.
[151:50] Oh, I made a quarter million dollars last year, bro. Okay, cool, man. love to hear that. And then in your calculator while you're talking, $250,000 divided by 12 months divided by 30 days divided by 24 hours.
[152:15] That's how much money that person is literally making every single hour. Whether they're taking a shit sleeping, fucking working, doesn't matter. They're getting paid whatever that is. $14.82 every single hour, no matter what.
[152:40] Then you can ask the golden question. How much time are you spending doing ads? Let's say on a monthly basis right now. Oh, I'm spending uh, you know, not much, about six hours a month.
[153:00] Okay, sounds good. And then you just do the math and you figure out, okay, you've been spending literally thousands of dollars of your time solving ad problems.
[153:20] Now, if you're $10,000 a month, and that still is way more than what they're actually spending in time, they have to understand that they're not out of pocket $10,000 by working.
[153:40] They're only out of pocket the difference between the amount of time they're spending currently doing it on their own and what they're purchasing that time back for with the remaining balance of that 10k.
[154:00] So don't I mean you tell people yeah 10k a month but at the end of the day I'd love to figure out what that really costs your business.
[154:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Time Buyback ROI Calculation"
NÚMEROS: 10 horas/mês recuperadas, $3,500/hora (valor do tempo), $35,000 (valor total recuperado) vs. $10,000 (investimento)
VISUAL: ROI de compra de tempo: 10 horas × $3,500/hora = $35,000 de valor vs. $10,000 de custo, conceito de encontrar dinheiro no calendário
[154:35] And by the way, a lot of the people that you guys are working with that are doing their ads by themselves or landing pages or whatever, it's probably not likely that running ads or doing copy or doing email campaigns or any of the little specific things that you guys do.
[155:00] It's highly unlikely that that is the number one best thing for them to be doing in their business is they think of you better than anybody else. Which means that their copy is broken, means that their email campaigns and segmentation is broken. means that their ads are fucking broken.
[155:25] Can you really afford to continue doing it by yourself? One, knowing that you're not really optimized, and knowing two, it's costing you thousands of dollars a month of your own time anyway to go and do this.
[155:50] If I could put an extra 10 hours a month in your back pocket, what would you do with it? Would you go spend more time with your wife and kids? Would you do that extra round of golf that you would like to do on a Friday morning?
[156:15] Would you work back on the business and trip and fall over the extra six grand that it's you're investing into buying that time back?
[156:35] Like, if you can put 10 hours of actual real work time back in my back pocket, calculated out at about 3,500 bucks an hour, holy shit bro. I just found $35,000 of potential revenue uh creation in my own calendar.
[157:00] So, I should easily be able to make sense out of writing you a check for $10,000. And that's also too when you guys are closing people, this is a bit how you can get to a non-emotional conversation about the 10.
[157:20] Most people, they're not used to paying 10 and the ad spend. At least in my experience working with other people, most people are like, dude, you charge 10 grand to run my ads and then I got to pay for ad spend.
[157:40] It's like, well, dude, let let's let's look at the math and figure out if we can become unemotional about this. If I could put an extra 35 grand of time back into your pocket. Fuck fuck the rorowaz. Let's watch sales go down a little bit because I just put 35 grand back in your pocket by you giving me 10.
[158:10] I know what I would do. I want my time. It's the same thing on like you guys can all do this exercise too. You can uh think on how much you earned last year. Divide that number by 12. Divide it by 30 days in in a month. Divide that by 24 hours.
[158:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Laundry Example - Time Value"
NÚMEROS: 3 horas/semana (lavanderia), $1,000/hora (valor do tempo exemplo), $3,000/semana, $12,000/mês (custo de tempo fazendo lavanderia), $25/ano (custo de Norma a lavadeira)
VISUAL: Exemplo prático: 3h/semana de lavanderia × $1,000/hora = $12,000/mês desperdiçados vs. $25/ano para terceirizar, matemática clara de delegação
[158:50] You'll figure out what you're actually worth every hour. whether you're sleeping, working, whatever. And then uh think about some fucking activity that you you do and you don't really like doing laundry, maybe.
[159:15] Maybe you're spending uh three hours a week putting laundry in the washer, taking out putting in the dryer, taking it out, folding your your underwear nicely, and putting it in your shelves and on the hanger, and you've done your laundry.
[159:40] Well, let's just say that you're worth $1,000 an hour and you're spending three hours a week doing laundry. That's $3,000 per week. You're throwing down the drain of your time doing something you don't get any satisfaction or fulfillment from.
[160:05] So 3,000 times four weeks a month, that's $12,000 a month that you're spending doing something that you hate doing.
[160:20] What would you rather have? The the money to pay like my girl Norma who's $25 a year to do your laundry for you so that you can either divide your time into enjoyment and fulfillment.
[160:40] or I can trip and fall over the money I pay Norma if I apply 10 minutes into making a post with one CTA, sell a couple decks of cash cards and pay Norma's laundry bill and then I get all my I get 12 hours a month back.
[161:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Rich People Do Nothing Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de que ricos "não fazem nada" porque delegaram tudo que não querem fazer, aparência de inatividade vs. realidade de foco estratégico
[161:20] And that's that's why you see rich people doing nothing. It's not that they do nothing. It's just that they don't fucking want to. Why do I want to do this shit Like it's why I don't cut my grass. I don't clean my house. I don't feed I don't I'm hard I hardly even feed myself.
[161:45] Like quick question. How many of you guys if they made a pill and they made a pill that you could take that would that would allow you to live a completely normal life but you would never be required to actually eat for nutritional purposes. How many of you guys would actually take that pill?
[162:10] I got two hands up. A lot of you guys would. But that means also here's the back side of it. How many of you guys would still take that pill, but like you couldn't eat a steak again or like your mom's home cooking or like how many of you guys would take that pill?
[162:35] I still do it all day long. Like I've like some Australian Wagu and like you know Jeremy took me out to Alania or whatever it's called in Chicago. Three star Michelin. I'd probably regret taking that pill if he took me there again.
[163:00] And I was like, "Wait a second. I can't can't eat any of this." Cuz that was that was flagging. Uh, by the way, get richer, man. Jeremy treated me to a hell of a dinner, bro. I appreciate that.
[163:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What's Your Time Actually Worth?"
NÚMEROS: 4 horas (trabalho focado vs. 8 horas no escritório), $3 milhões/ano (receita atual), 20 meses (tempo para construir), 300% crescimento YoY
VISUAL: Filosofia de otimização de tempo: 4 horas focadas = 8 horas desperdiçadas, resultados: $3M em 20 meses através de delegação radical
[163:35] Um, but the point is is what's your time actually worth? You're spending it either way. And also in your office and what you're doing, if you really are worth $1,000 an hour, don't spend eight hours in the fucking office.
[164:00] Spend the four hours you know you need to be focused in and get that work done and then go do whatever the hell you want to do. you're getting the same amount of actual work done.
[164:20] And that's why I've been able to scale pretty quickly in not even 24 months. In about 20 months, multi-million about $3 million a year right now. Um, which is semi-embarrassing to say. Should be a lot better.
[164:45] Uh, but I did that in 20 months because I'm constantly like, dude, what can I get off my plate? Yes, I like landing pages, but who the fuck can I call that can do those for me? Yes, I like editing videos. It's a pretty cool little creative process that I like to do. Who can do it for me? It's a total waste of time.
[165:15] I don't need you spending four hours doing this. And so, really look at all the things that you do because here's the thing that I want to get at is I've been studying rich, wealthy, affluent people for a long time.
[165:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Stop Adding, Start Subtracting"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Filosofia fundamental: não adicione o que ricos fazem ao que você já faz, subtraia o que eles NÃO fazem, conceito de crescimento por eliminação
[165:55] And a lot of us, we look at the Jeff Bezos out there and the Warren Buffetts and, you know, the Jeremy Haynes out there, people that have what we don't. And then and then we learn some of the things that they do and we're like, "Wait a second. If I just add those things to everything else that I'm doing, I'm going to get rich." Like, "No, you're fucking not."
[166:25] Because one of the reasons why you're not doing some of the things that they're doing is because you're doing a bunch of other shit.
[166:40] So, if you want to really study the wealthy, the successful, and the the version of the people you actually want to be, whatever those numbers that you wrote down yesterday, like I did when Jeremy was lighting a candle underneath our all of our asses.
[167:00] is stop thinking about what else you need to do. Start thinking about all the shit you shouldn't be doing. Period. Should be saying mo no to mostly everything that doesn't fit in that top left quadrant of what it is that you're doing.
[167:25] In order to really successfully grow your business, you're gonna have to bite the bullet, hire some people, learn some hard lessons that that person wasn't a good fit. You'll go backwards a little bit.
[167:45] I spent the last five months literally going through that process of fucking man. It's time to make some bigger moves, make some bigger investments. I learned took a couple little L's on the way.
[168:05] Luckily, five years of business before doing all that primed me to make better right decisions than than whatever. But at the end of the day, some of you might be in that stage in your business where you're like, "Things are going pretty good, but it's kind of plateaued a little bit.
[168:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "5% Growth = 5% Decline Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: 5% up vs. 5% down (mesma coisa), 500% crescimento (objetivo vs. 5%), 250% (resultado realista após ajustes), $10 milhões (meta anual)
VISUAL: Conceito radical: 5% de crescimento é tão inútil quanto 5% de queda, meta de 500% para alcançar 250%, rejeição de crescimento incremental
[168:45] Maybe things are improving a little bit, but it's not very exciting." A little bit of growth is just as fucking great as a little bit of down as a little bit of a decrease. It's the same shit. Doesn't really take any food off anybody's plates. No lives are being changed. Nothing's happening.
[169:10] It's just kind of like, "Yeah, we're up 5%." It's the same fucking as being down 5%. Well, I mean, unless you're running a multi-billion dollar organization, that 5% means a load of money.
[169:30] But in our businesses, everyone included, it's like, dude, 5% down is just as good as 5% up. So, why not go 500% up and and trip and fall and end up at 250 for the year?
[169:55] Like, I would like to grow two and a half times this year. That'd be phenomenal. Put me in 10 million this year. That's actually what I wrote down yesterday. I got a lot of work to do.
[170:15] So the point is is that I'm constantly looking at dude what what can I get off my plate? And it's a heavy conversation that all of you guys need to have with yourselves, your business partners, the people you spend your time with, you know, it's just uh it's an very important thing.
[170:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Kids and Time Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: 8-9 meses (idade da filha), 90 graus (temperatura da piscina), primeira aula de natação
VISUAL: Filosofia pessoal sobre família: perder momentos agora para garantir futuro melhor, amor através de provisão, balanço entre presença e construção de legado
[170:55] How many of you guys have kids? Couple of you guys. Okay. So it's like when the rest of y'all do have kids or if and for those of you that do have kids, it's like, bro, I have to look at that right now.
[171:15] I have an eight well nine months I think today. nine-month-old. Dude, I really want to be this morning. She did her first swimming lesson, bro. She was in the the 90 degree pool with my wife and like in her first fucking swimming lesson.
[171:40] I'm like, "Dude, they're fucking dope." My wife sent me some photos. I'm like, "Dude, I missed it." But she ain't going to remember the fact that I wasn't there. And I got fucking work to do because I love my daughter enough to miss some of those moments.
[172:05] And that is a value system I've created for myself so I can have a compass when it comes time to figure out what I need to do for my family because I'm the person in the family that provides for the family.
[172:25] and also I'm not going to give up my personal dreams because like I'm just going to fulfill my family. A lot of a lot of you guys love everybody to think in terms of or I can send my daughter to private, you know, private school or I could buy a Ferrari and be like Jeremy. It's like, fuck that. How about both?
[172:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Who Not How Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: 8 anos (tempo para $10M sem ajuda) vs. 2 anos (com coaching/contratação), $100,000 (investimento em coach exemplo)
VISUAL: Mudança de mentalidade: parar de perguntar "como" e começar a perguntar "quem", referência ao livro "Who Not How", conceito de aceleração através de expertise externa
[173:10] And then stop asking how you're going to do something and start asking who's going to do it for you. You should write that down.
[173:25] When you guys find yourself trying to figure out how you're going to get to 10 million a year, how you're going to do X, Y, and Z, ask yourself, who's either going to do it for me or who can help teach me to go?
[173:50] Because, as we already mentioned, you'll figure it out. I will no doubt get to $10 million a year in in in this one business venture that I have. I already know it.
[174:10] Like uh Brandon Carter was saying yesterday, dude, I wish a star would fucking tell me that I am going to go there or not. I don't care. I'm going to put in the work. I'm going to make it impossible to fail and I'll get there.
[174:30] But what if if I'm just going through the motions, what if it takes me eight years to do $10 million? But if I would have invested $100,000 into the right coach or the right employee or the right person that could get me there in two years, what would you rather do?
[174:55] wait eight years to hit that goal or wait two and lose a couple of bucks. Obviously, you're going to outsource that to the who that can either teach you to do it and has already been there.
[175:15] In which case, if you're doing $10 million a year, your time is probably pretty valuable, which means that I'm going to have to pay a pretty good chunk of money for that uh info. But you've done it. You have the info. Now I just apply it to what I do and I can make the 10 million.
[175:40] or I hire somebody else out there to go and do whatever it is that I need to do so that it doesn't require my time or my experience level in order to hit it.
[176:00] So that's also a Dan Sullivan thing I got to give him credit to. It's actually a book uh who not how great book. I'm a big fan of that.
[176:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sell The Way You Buy - Final Reminder"
NÚMEROS: $54,000 (exemplo ZoomInfo), $250,000 (investimento em tech), decisões sem consultar cônjuge
VISUAL: Reforço do conceito: suas objeções pessoais criam objeções em vendas, exemplo de decisões unilaterais de alto valor, mentalidade de comprador confiante
[176:35] Um and so that that's my other piece. I don't even know whose question I just answered with that. But I do have a little bit more time. I don't know if the food is ready. I'm waiting for Jeremy to tell me yes or no. But I can do some more Q&A. Sales, business, entrepreneurship, whatever you guys got. Mindset.
[177:00] question on your tech stack. I know you use Shopify for the like cash cards. Is pretty much everything else done through HubSpot in terms of like blogs, mass email that way, the lead score, can we track that way or are you using like the code that you had mentioned on?
[177:25] It's a it's a lot of code. Uh it's code driven a lot. Um and you can drop code into multiple domains. So like I have stupar sales academy for individual clients on Kajabi like all those different pages or whatever. I have the code in that so that I can tell if you're on jordanstupar.com looking for cash cards or if you got kicked over to the sales academy or whatever those.
[177:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lead Scoring Optimization"
NÚMEROS: Ajustes contínuos, nunca perfeito mas sempre melhorando
VISUAL: Processo de otimização contínua de lead scoring, ajustes baseados em esquecimentos (email bounce = lead morto), adição de novos formulários e landing pages
[178:10] we we attempted to build it out as good as we could. Um, and you know, you always drop the ball or forget, you know, something important where like if their email bounces, it's like you might want to subtract points and turn them into a dead lead so that you can keep up your email deliverability, yada yada.
[178:35] And then it's like, oh, let's put that in or I'll create a new form or landing page for something else. And then I know that this is weighted differently. That's where we'll make tweaks.
[178:50] Like I said, it's probably never going to be perfect. Uh, but we'll optimize as we can go and get it as perfect as possible.
[179:10] We got blueberry. You want to do one more? Let's do one more high quality question. Is it a good one? Let's fucking go.
[179:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Onboarding New Sales Reps - Priority Framework"
